beats.prompt: Quick primer — what probability means and how to reduce a fraction before counting hands.

interaction.title: P = favorable ÷ total
interaction.body: For equally-likely outcomes (e.g. all 5-card hands from a shuffled deck), P(event) = count of favorable outcomes ÷ count of all outcomes. Reduce to lowest terms by dividing both by their GCD. Example: 624 / 2,598,960 → GCD = 624 → 1/4165.

feedback.correct: P(event) = favorable count ÷ total count, reduced to lowest terms.
hints: Count the outcomes you want; count all possible outcomes.
To reduce: divide numerator and denominator by their GCD. Example: 624 / 2,598,960 → GCD=624 → 1/4165.
Continue to the counting beats.

beats.prompt: Retrieve the probabilities you've already calculated — each is a favorable ÷ total.

feedback.correct: Both probabilities and the formula — each a favorable ÷ total.
hints: Tap a label, then its match.
Dice strictly-increasing (L2): 20 / 216 = 5/54. P(event) = favorable ÷ total.

beats.prompt: Gut check only — rank these three poker hands from rarest to most common: four-of-a-kind, full house, two pairs.

Four-of-a-kind → full house → two pairs.note: Good gut — but a hunch isn't proof. Let's count each hand and watch the ranking hold up.

Two pairs → full house → four-of-a-kind.note: Let's test it — counting may flip your gut. We'll divide each count by the total and compare.

Full house → four-of-a-kind → two pairs.note: Let's test it — feel isn't enough; the counts pin down the true order.

beats.prompt: In L4 you counted 624 four-of-a-kind hands. C(52,5) = 2,598,960 total 5-card hands exist. What is P(four of a kind)?

feedback.correct: Exactly — 624 / 2,598,960 reduces to 1/4165. The count became a probability with one division.
hints: 624 is the *count*, not the odds. A probability is favorable ÷ total — divide 624 by 2,598,960.
624 / 2,598,960 — reduce by dividing both by their GCD (624): 1/4165.
1/4165. One division turns the count into the probability.

beats.prompt: Tap each factor chip to include it in the full house count. Watch the count build and the fraction live-reduce.

hints: Tap each chip to multiply it into the favorable count.
13 · 4 · 12 · 6 = 3744 full house hands.

beats.interviewNote: The shared denominator 4165 is exact: gcd(2,598,960, 624)=624 → 1/4165; gcd(2,598,960, 3744)=624 → 6/4165; gcd(2,598,960, 123,552)=624 → 198/4165. Over denominator 4165, ranking reduces to numerator order: 1 < 6 < 198 → four-of-a-kind < full house < two pairs. GB p.34 §4.2.
beats.prompt: Tap each lens to see the same formula applied three ways.

lenses.label: The Formula
lenses.body: For equally-likely outcomes: P(event) = favorable count ÷ total count. Reduce to lowest terms with the GCD.

lenses.label: Full House
lenses.body: Favorable: 3744. Total: 2,598,960. P = 3744 / 2,598,960 = 6/4165. Both fractions share denominator 4165.

feedback.correct: Same formula, same denominator — count, then divide, then compare.
hints: Each example applies P = favorable ÷ total.
Shared denominator 4165: ranking is purely by numerator.

beats.prompt: Rank these two hands from rarest to most common. Tap a hand to place it in order, or use Up/Down arrow keys to reorder.

feedback.correct: Right — same denominator (4165), so the bigger top wins: 6/4165 beats 1/4165. Full house is more common.
hints: Bigger numerator means *more* common, not rarer. Over 4165, 6 outranks 1 — full house beats four-of-a-kind.
Four of a kind: 1/4165. Full house: 6/4165. Same denominator — compare the tops.
Rarest first: four of a kind (1/4165), then full house (6/4165).

beats.prompt: Before the mastery challenge: build the two-pairs count. C(13,2)=78 chooses the two pair-ranks; C(4,2)=6 picks each rank's suits; 44 is the kicker count.

hints: Two pair-ranks chosen unordered: C(13,2) = 78.
Each rank's suit choice: C(4,2) = 6; both together = 36.
Kicker: any card not of the two chosen ranks = 44. 78 · 36 · 44 = 123,552.

beats.prompt: From scratch: how many two-pairs hands are in a 5-card deck? Then find P(two pairs). Finally, confirm the full ranking: four-of-a-kind, full house, two pairs.

interaction.scenario: Choose \(2\) ranks unordered — \(\binom{13}{2}\) ways; pick the suits for each rank's pair — \(\binom{4}{2}\) each; then choose a kicker from the \(44\) remaining cards (not of either chosen rank).

beats.interviewNote: P(two pairs) = 123,552/2,598,960 = 198/4165 (GB p.34 §4.2). Count: C(13,2)·C(4,2)²·44 = 78·6·6·44 = 123,552; the '44 = 52 − 8' kicker bars both chosen ranks' 8 cards. GB OCR garbles printed integer to '123,582'; formula 78×6×6×44 = 123,552 unambiguous (engine: probabilityFromCounts(123552, 2598960) = 198/4165).

feedback.correct: Count step by step: \[ \binom{13}{2} \cdot \binom{4}{2}^2 \cdot 44 = 78 \cdot 6 \cdot 6 \cdot 44 = 123{,}552. \] \(\binom{13}{2} = 78\): choose the two pair-ranks unordered. \(\binom{4}{2} = 6\): pick which 2 of 4 suits form each rank's pair, applied twice. \(44 = 52 - 8\): kicker is any card not of either chosen rank. The probability is \[ P(\text{two pairs}) = \frac{123{,}552}{2{,}598{,}960} = \frac{198}{4165}. \] Over the shared denominator 4165 the numerators rank \(1 < 6 < 198\) — two pairs is the most common of the three.
hints: The two ranks are unordered — 'kings & sevens' equals 'sevens & kings.' Use \(\binom{13}{2}\), not \(13 \times 12\).
\(\binom{13}{2} \cdot \binom{4}{2}^2 \cdot 44 = 78 \cdot 6 \cdot 6 \cdot 44 = 123{,}552\) two-pairs hands.

beats.prompt: Tap through the recap cards — count → divide → rank closes the Combinatorics concept.

feedback.correct: Count, divide, rank — the probability engine closes the Combinatorics arc.
hints: Count favorable outcomes with multiplication and combinations.
Divide by C(52,5) = 2,598,960 to convert a count into a probability.
Shared denominator 4165: rank by numerator — 1 < 6 < 198.
